column2: Question to Ask
column4: Customer Answer

column2: "How do you usually describe your space in the market?"
column4: Medical distributor. They also manufacture their own product line, repair sterilizers, and refurbish sterilizers. So manufacturing, distribution, and service.

column2: "Do you manufacture, distribute, or both?"
column4: All of the above. Distribution, in house product line, repairs, refurbishing services.

column2: "Was QuickBooks integration a key factor in choosing Method?"
column4: Yes. Customizable platform plus QuickBooks sync were the two main reasons for choosing Method.

column2: "Why keep QuickBooks instead of moving to ERP?"
column4: Leadership preference. Their boss prefers QuickBooks. AP is managed there.

column2: "What made you switch to Method from your previous CRM?"
column4: Customization and QuickBooks sync. They previously used Legrand as CRM.

column2: "How customized is your Method account?"
column4: Highly customized. “Alex is constantly customizing to our needs.” They do not use a basic setup.

column2: "How do teams use Method day to day?"
column4: Universal usage across sales, shipping, customer care, AP, AR. Used for leads, sales orders, invoices, quotes, activity notes, calendar events, manual payments.

column2: "How do you handle payments?"
column4: Manual payments entered in Method. Credit card and wire payments processed in QuickBooks. Accounting reports mostly run out of QuickBooks.

column2: "Do you use Method reporting?"
column4: Limited. Reporting mainly done in QuickBooks. They find Method reporting less intuitive. Want product level YoY reporting options.

column2: "How do you track inventory?"
column4: Inventory primarily managed in QuickBooks. No Fishbowl or external inventory system.

column2: "How is Method email marketing working for you?"
column4: Major frustration. Email blasts over ~3,000 contacts cause suspension. Must send in batches of 800. Wants list based sending, segmentation without manual labeling, and better email analytics such as clicks and conversions.

column2: "Any operational friction?"
column4: Heavy customization makes system slow. Needs to reboot when new customizations are added.

column2: "How is the QuickBooks sync?"
column4: Still has to manually re run integrations at times. Mentions glitches.

column2: Customer evidence quote
column4: Marketing verbiage

column1: Email marketing is their biggest friction point and a retention risk
column2: “Anytime I do an email blast… I get put on suspension… it’s not sustainable.”
column3: Email capacity limits and suspension issues are a real pain. They want to stay in one system but are forced to use SendGrid or Mailchimp.
column4: If you promise all in one, it has to scale.

column1: They want CRM visibility tied directly to email activity
column2: “What I love about it… it’ll say that they received it.”
column3: The appeal of in platform email is contact level visibility. They want attribution inside CRM.
column4: See marketing activity inside the customer record.

column1: They operate a hybrid manufacturing, distribution, and service model
column2: “We’re medical distributor. We have our own product line. We repair sterilizers. We refurbish.”
column3: This is a complex MWD plus Field Service hybrid ICP. Sales plus service plus product lifecycle tracking matters.
column4: Built for businesses that sell, ship, and service.

column1: Their Method instance is heavily customized and actively maintained
column2: “Alex is constantly customizing to our needs.”
column3: They rely on customization as an operational backbone. They are not using Method as a lightweight CRM.
column4: Adapt the system as your business evolves.

column2: “Sales side, shipping side, customer care, AP, AR… we kind of use it all the same.”
column3: Method acts as operational front end while QuickBooks remains accounting engine.
column4: One operational system across departments.

column1: They use Method for operational workflow but trust QuickBooks for financial reporting
column2: “I believe Greg creates all the reports out of QuickBooks.”
column3: Accounting authority still sits in QuickBooks. Method is execution layer.
column4: Operational control on top. Accounting source of truth underneath.

column2: “The more we make it customizable, it becomes heavy and slower.”
column3: Power users notice performance degradation. Scale and performance optimization matters for advanced accounts.
column4: Power should not slow you down.

column1: Migration from QuickBooks Desktop influenced CRM selection
column2: “QuickBooks desktop was one of the best features… QuickBooks online is horrible.”
column3: Dissatisfaction with QBO experience may increase dependency on Method for structure and control.
column4: When QuickBooks changes, your workflow should not break.

column2: “Because it was customizable and it synced with QuickBooks.”
column3: Confirms our core positioning pillars for manufacturing ICP.
column4: Custom CRM built around QuickBooks.

column1: They want product level and year over year product performance reporting
column2: “More options for reports… for products… this year versus last year.”
column3: Product intelligence is important for a distributor manufacturer. Inventory plus reporting opportunity.
column4: Understand product performance without exporting spreadsheets.

column1: They want list based segmentation for email without manual tagging
column2: “If I upload a list… I should be able to say give me all New York.”
column3: Segmentation UX matters. Manual labeling feels clunky and inefficient.
column4: Segment and send in seconds, not hours.

column1: They experience occasional sync glitches and need to re run integrations
column2: “I always have to do the integrations.”
column3: Reliability still impacts perception. Even minor friction affects trust.
column4: Sync that just works.
